Data Context: RFID/customer-flow data from Flavoria self-service restaurant, University of Turku, 2019–2024, enriched with weather, holidays, statistical, and menu features.
Models / Methodology Used: Linear Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, LSTM
Main Finding / Contribution: XGBoost achieved the strongest performance. Previous-day demand, exponential smoothing, holidays, day of week, and weather were influential.
Gap or Limitation: Single university self-service restaurant; ML-focused; no SARIMAX; not multi-location.
What We Implemented: We adapted the logic of temporal, weather, holiday, event, lag, and rolling features into an interpretable POS-based forecasting framework.
Methodological Improvement: We combined interpretable SARIMAX structure with Random Forest residual correction instead of relying only on black-box ML.
Empirical Evidence / Result: Baseline MAPE improved from 22.48% to 16.20% with the Hybrid SARIMAX + RF model.
Dashboard Evidence: Model Evaluation & Validation; Feature Engineering & Selection

Research Focus: Restaurant store management based on demand forecasting
Data Context: Restaurant chain data using POS data and external data such as weather and events.
Evaluation Metrics: Forecasting accuracy for customer order quantity and inventory order quantity.
Main Finding / Contribution: Forecasting can support employee placement and food material ordering.
Gap or Limitation: Uses ML rather than SARIMAX; limited emphasis on interpretability.
What We Implemented: We connected forecast results to staffing readiness, menu preparation, inventory planning, promotion timing, and operational recommendations.
Methodological Improvement: We moved from pure forecasting toward a decision-support dashboard for restaurant managers.
Empirical Evidence / Result: Dashboard provides operational interpretation, KPI cards, final holdout forecast, and manager-facing decision support.

Research Focus: Food and beverage sales prediction in restaurants and staff canteens
Data Context: POS data from a casual restaurant and a large staff canteen over approximately 20 months.
Evaluation Metrics: Predictive comparison against established forecasting approaches.
Main Finding / Contribution: Interpretable POS-based models can handle multiple seasonalities, gaps, trend changes, and outliers.
Gap or Limitation: Limited emphasis on external variables such as weather, events, holidays, or promotions.
What We Implemented: We integrated POS history with calendar, seasonality, weather exposure, event indicators, holidays, lags, rolling averages, and KPI features.
Methodological Improvement: We expanded the modeling pipeline from POS-only forecasting to POS plus external-variable and KPI-aware forecasting.
Empirical Evidence / Result: Final modeling dataset includes 474 daily observations and 50 modeling variables; enhanced KPI layer includes 115 candidate engineered variables.
Dashboard Evidence: Data Pipeline & Data Quality; Feature Engineering & Selection

Research Focus: Survey of machine learning techniques for food sales prediction
Data Context: Broad literature survey across food-related businesses such as supermarkets, grocery stores, restaurants, bakeries, and patisseries.
Evaluation Metrics: Review of accuracy measures across prior studies.
Main Finding / Contribution: Highlights key design decisions: granularity, input variables, target representation, algorithms, and evaluation metrics.
Gap or Limitation: Broad survey; no direct restaurant SARIMAX implementation; no specific applied restaurant dashboard.
What We Implemented: We operationalized these design decisions into a structured data pipeline, target definition, daily aggregation, feature engineering process, model comparison, and validation strategy.
Methodological Improvement: We transformed broad forecasting design principles into an applied restaurant POS forecasting workflow.
Empirical Evidence / Result: Pipeline transforms 276,109 cleaned POS records and 81 source columns into daily modeling, validation, and dashboard-ready datasets.
Dashboard Evidence: Data Pipeline & Data Quality; Analytics Roadmap

Research Focus: Review of forecasting studies for the restaurant industry
Data Context: Literature review of restaurant forecasting studies focused on results, contributions, and limitations.
Main Finding / Contribution: Restaurant forecasting studies often focus on model building and accuracy; hybrid models are common.
Gap or Limitation: No new empirical model or dataset; practical decision support remains underdeveloped.
What We Implemented: We moved beyond accuracy metrics by deploying an interactive dashboard that connects forecasting outputs with operational interpretation.
Methodological Improvement: We translated model evaluation into manager-facing decision support rather than stopping at model performance.
Empirical Evidence / Result: QR-accessible Streamlit dashboard includes research framework, pipeline, model results, and executive decision dashboard.
Dashboard Evidence: Literature Gap & Contribution; Executive Decision Dashboard

Research Focus: Restaurant demand forecasting using POS data from a large restaurant chain
Data Context: POS plus external data, including sales, promotions, macroeconomic, and pandemic-related variables.
Models / Methodology Used: Random Forest, XGBoost, RNN, Transformer-based models
Main Finding / Contribution: Advanced ML and deep learning models can improve forecasting accuracy; reported up to 95% improvement.
Gap or Limitation: Strong focus on model performance; limited interpretability and practical implementation for managers.
What We Implemented: We prioritized interpretability through SARIMAX, then added Random Forest residual correction only after establishing the statistical forecasting core.
Methodological Improvement: We balanced interpretability and accuracy instead of relying only on complex deep learning architectures.
Empirical Evidence / Result: Baseline MAPE 22.48%; SARIMAX Basic MAPE 18.50%; Hybrid SARIMAX + RF MAPE 16.20%.

Research Focus: Short-term demand forecasting in food catering services
Data Context: Transactional catering demand data enriched with temporal and external variables such as weather.
Models / Methodology Used: Random Forest, LightGBM, LSTM, Transformer models
Main Finding / Contribution: Short-term demand forecasting can reduce overproduction and food waste by approximately 14%–52%.
Gap or Limitation: Focuses mainly on catering and food waste reduction, with less emphasis on broader restaurant decision support.
What We Implemented: We adapted short-term forecasting logic to restaurant POS sales, transactions, average ticket, and operational planning.
Methodological Improvement: We extended the operational value from waste reduction to staffing, preparation, inventory readiness, and promotional timing.
Empirical Evidence / Result: Manager dashboard shows net sales, transactions, average ticket, annual behavior, holdout forecast, and operational value.

Research Focus: Restaurant sales forecasting using POS data
Data Context: Restaurant POS data with time-based variables such as trends and seasonality.
Models / Methodology Used: Linear Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, Recurrent Neural Networks
Main Finding / Contribution: Machine learning models outperformed simpler baselines and sales forecasting can support employee scheduling.
What We Implemented: We built a framework that can be extended to multi-location forecasting, while the current MVP focuses on Twisted Bar because it has the most complete dataset.
Methodological Improvement: We made the scope decision transparent: Twisted Bar is the complete MVP; Chile & Agave remains a future extension due to partial data availability.
Empirical Evidence / Result: Current MVP uses complete Twisted Bar data; future roadmap includes multi-location expansion.

Research Focus: Comparative study of statistical and machine learning models for restaurant sales forecasting
Models / Methodology Used: Simple Exponential Smoothing, Random Forest, XGBoost, Multilayer Perceptron
Main Finding / Contribution: Machine learning models can outperform statistical approaches, but interpretability remains an important trade-off.
Gap or Limitation: Accuracy and interpretability trade-off remains unresolved for practical restaurant management.
What We Implemented: We compared baseline, SARIMAX, and hybrid models to balance explainability and predictive improvement.
Methodological Improvement: We used SARIMAX as an interpretable core and Random Forest as a residual correction layer.
Empirical Evidence / Result: SARIMAX Basic MAPE 18.50%; Hybrid SARIMAX + RF MAPE 16.20%.

Research Focus: Stacked ensemble model for food demand forecasting
Data Context: Food demand data with sales, prices, promotions, and product characteristics.
Models / Methodology Used: Stacked ensemble using Random Forest, XGBoost, Support Vector Regression, and LSTM
Main Finding / Contribution: Stacked ensemble achieved very high predictive accuracy, with R² close to 0.99.
Gap or Limitation: High model complexity may limit implementation in small and medium-sized restaurants.
What We Implemented: We developed a more practical forecasting workflow that is easier to explain, deploy, and connect with operational decisions.
Methodological Improvement: We used hybrid modeling only where it added value: Random Forest corrects SARIMAX residuals instead of replacing the entire approach with a complex ensemble.
Empirical Evidence / Result: Hybrid model improved accuracy while preserving an interpretable forecasting structure.
